name: Thi Há
animal-fact: The Costasiella Sea Slug, also known as the 'leaf sheep' drifts through life, unconcerned by the world around it. It is one of the only known animals that is part plant. Rather than digesting the sea-grass it eats, it absorbs its food into its body and grows with it, using it to photosynthesise.

intro.speaker: You
intro.text: Hey Thi Há.

intro.speaker: You
intro.text: How’s it going? I like your hair, very... bold.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: Hey sweetheart, it’s been ages!

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: Look at you, you’re all grown up now!

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: It’s been so long, in my mind you’re still that little kid running around my garden with Louis during the holidays.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: Have you said hi to Louis yet?

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: I think he missed you these last couple years.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: How have you been?

intro.speaker: You
intro.text: Yeah, me and Louis have talked.

intro.speaker: You
intro.text: I’ve been good. Studying musical composition in Uni... it’s been fun.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: That’s so exciting.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: I was quite the musician myself when I was younger, mostly piano, though I quite liked the saxophone too.

intro.speaker: You
intro.text: I remember. You used to teach me a bit of piano when I was younger.

intro.speaker: You
intro.text: Don’t remember much of the saxophone though,

intro.speaker: You
intro.text: Do you still play it?

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: Oh no.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: I used to love it, but I gave it up after my husband died.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: I’ve gotten very into Buddhist ideologies, and part of it is the idea of letting go of worldly possessions.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: To attach your sense of self and happiness to nothing external.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: And when you find yourself sad or overwhelmed you just draw a deep breath and meditate until you find peace again.

intro.speaker: You
intro.text: Right... sounds... legitimate...

intro.speaker: You
intro.text: What’s Louis think about this?

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: Oh, he doesn’t quite get it, but he’s young.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: Hides away in his room instead of sitting down to meditate with me.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: But everyone's journey to enlightenment is their own, I can’t rush him.

intro.speaker: You
intro.text: I’m kind of a hypocrite is asking this, but are you saying you didn’t talk to Louis about his dad, y’know, dying? At all?

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: Oh honey, I was in a very dark place when it happened.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: But I found peace through realising the world simply is as it is, and moving with it rather than against it.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: Louis wasn’t particularly open to my guidance, so I’m waiting for him to find his own way.

intro.speaker: You
intro.text: Wow, really giving it your all, aren’t you?

intro.speaker: You
intro.text: I’m sure he feels very supported.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: ... I don’t appreciate your tone, dear.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: And I don’t appreciate your lying, either.

intro.speaker: Thi Há
intro.text: I don’t think you’ll be doing that to me again at all.

intro.speaker: You
intro.text: ... did you just curse me?

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: You used FACTS AND LOGIC

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: You’re talking about letting go of worldly attachments, but you’re still wearing all those gold bracelets?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: Why does this seem more like an aesthetic choice than an actual commitment to inner-peace?

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: You used FACTS AND LOGIC

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: What’s with all the crystal healing stuff?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: That’s not even Buddhist, it’s like, Ancient Egyptian and Mesopatamian or something isn’t it?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: Looks to me like you’re just mixing and matching random ‘mystic’ bullshit!

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: You used FACTS AND LOGIC

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: Why the fuck do you know voodoo and witchcraft anyway?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: Like, I know you’ve got an aesthetic going on, but that’s a bizarre choice.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: Why did you learn how to curse people?

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: You used EMOTIONAL MANIPULATION

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: It’s super effective!

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: You left your own son in the lurch after his dad died.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: Don’t you feel some shame? Or responsibility?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: Louis’s literally caving his face in doing bone-smashing because you’ve been so on your enlightenment bullshit that you aren’t paying attention to him at all.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: You used EMOTIONAL MANIPULATION

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: It’s super effective!

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: I feel bad for Louis, y’know.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: It’s hard enough losing one parent, let alone both at the same time.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: You used EMOTIONAL MANIPULATION

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: It’s super effective!

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: I wonder what your husband would think of all this if he were still alive.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: He was a gentle, understanding and empathetic guy from what I remember... then again, so were you.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: I think he would be disappointed in how you’ve handled this.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: I think he might not even recognise who you are anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: You tried to use BLATANT LIE

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: You have been cursed and are currently unable to lie.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: You used UNNECESSARY ESCALATION

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: It wasn’t very effective

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: This whole look you’ve got going for you right now? Not good, I have to say.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: You’re dressed in vomit green and royal purple – those colours neither match, nor suit you.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: And your hair looks like it had a run in with a rogue hedge trimmer, it’s uneven as hell.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: This is just embarrassing.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: You used UNNECESSARY ESCALATION

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: This mysticism, hippy shit is, frankly, difficult to look at.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: No wonder Louis doesn’t go outside, I’d avoid people too if I was associated with you.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: You used UNNECESSARY ESCALATION

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: What’s your zodiac again? I bet you’re really into all that...

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: Let me guess... Libra! No... Cancer!

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: I mean that derogatorily, by the way.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: Thi Há used BRUTAL HONESTY!

dialogue.speaker: Thi Há
dialogue.text: You’re still hung up on it, aren’t you?

dialogue.speaker: Thi Há
dialogue.text: On the fact that your father left you the second he realised he could.

dialogue.speaker: Thi Há
dialogue.text: Do you still wonder if he loved you?

dialogue.speaker: Thi Há
dialogue.text: I don’t think he really could have, if he left like that.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: Jesus Christ, you didn’t even hesitate.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: Thi Há used ACTUAL REAL MAGIC!

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: It’s super effective!

dialogue.speaker: Thi Há
dialogue.text: Qiouwrsdaskhaorhkgsdjweydgabbca!

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: Ow, my head!

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: Did you just cast a spell at me?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: ... what the fuck?

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: Thi Há used ENLIGHTENMENT!

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: It’s not very effective

dialogue.speaker: Thi Há
dialogue.text: Your words are meaningless without me giving them weight.

dialogue.speaker: Thi Há
dialogue.text: You are but an external trigger for my internal struggles.

dialogue.speaker: Thi Há
dialogue.text: But I can control my own inner world.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: Is that Buddhism or Descartes?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: Because that’s some ‘I think therefore I am’ bullshit.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.text: Thi Há used DISMISSAL!

dialogue.speaker: Thi Há
dialogue.text: That’s very interesting of you to say.

dialogue.speaker: Thi Há
dialogue.text: Though it’s clear your inner turmoil is talking for you.

dialogue.speaker: Thi Há
dialogue.text: I shall listen to the meaning beyond your words.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.text: You’re just ignoring me and making up what you want me to say, aren’t you?

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: You seem very angry, sweetheart.

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: And a little bit confused.

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: Yeah I’m upset.

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: Your son’s literally getting radicalised and you're over here ignoring the problem... and learning magic?

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: I thought you were busy helping Louis and that’s why you never...

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: Never came around to visit after my dad left.

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: It’s hypocritical since I didn’t exactly visit you guys either, but... the reason mum didn’t take us up to see you guys was because she was so busy dealing the the fallout of dad.

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: She was focussing on me and Caspian.

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: I... I thought you were focusing on Louis...

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: It’s why I didn’t think about him that much... but if you weren’t.

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: What were you doing?

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: I know Uncle Quân and Lia made the rounds to both of us when everything happened, even though they suck at comforting people...

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: I... feel like the old you wouldn’t have done that...

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: I barely even recognise you, Thi Há. and it’s not because of the hair.

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: You know, there’s this thing in Buddhism called the Three Marks of Existence.

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: Anicca, impermanence, means that the world and everything in it is always changing. Both the physical and the mental world is ephemeral.

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: Dukkah, suffering, means that the world is inherently unsatisfactory, and there will always be suffering as the only constant in life.

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: And Anatta, non-self, meaning that the soul or self or ego really exists unchanged or is worth holding onto.

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: Perhaps I am different... and Louis’s changed too...

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: But it’s our attachments to those old selves that’s causing us harm.

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: That’s real lovely, except for the part where it means you don’t care that Louis is miserable and needs you to maintain at least your worldly attachment to him.

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: I think you’re projecting a little bit there, dear.

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: It’s not your father leaving or your mother lying that’s hurt you...

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: It’s your attachment to your father and your previous relationship,

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: It’s your attachment to the image of your mother you used to believe in,

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: And it’s your attachment to the image of yourself that you lost through the fallout that hurt you.

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: And it’s these ghosts of the past that are hurting your current relationships.

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: I don’t want to listen to your advice on relationships when your current approach seems to be to not care about them at all.

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: And what will you do instead?

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: Hang onto all your gripes like clutching at broken glass?

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: Pick fights with every single person you talk to because you don’t know how to let go of anything?

intermission-50%.speaker: You
intermission-50%.text: Can you not psycho analyse me?

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: Honey, I’m not trying

intermission-50%.speaker: Thi Há
intermission-50%.text: It’s just very obvious.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: Why are you actually upset?

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: You’ve been absent from the greater family get togethers for five years.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: You’re angry about Louis, like you didn’t ghost him since everything with your father happened.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: I’m just trying to find a little peace... is that so much to ask?

win.speaker: You
win.text: A little?

win.speaker: You
win.text: This sort of Buddhist detachment, divorce yourself from your worldly desires stuff is all fun and great if you’re a monk in a monastery,

win.speaker: You
win.text: But you’ve got people that are relying on you.

win.speaker: You
win.text: Louis’s seventeen, for god's sake, and you’re regarding him with the same ‘worldly attachment’ as the saxophone you got rid of!

win.speaker: You
win.text: It’s fucked up!

win.speaker: You
win.text: You can’t just... just leave people like that!

win.speaker: You
win.text: Even if you’re still actually present, you can’t just...

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: Oh sweetie, this isn’t like with your father.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: I’m not leaving Louis’s life... or yours.

win.speaker: You
win.text: Aren’t you?

win.speaker: You
win.text: I’m not exactly religious... and you’re not wrong when you say that most of my problems are because I can’t ever let anything go, but...

win.speaker: You
win.text: If you just let every problem go... doesn’t that just mean you’re not actually caring about the relationships?

win.speaker: You
win.text: You’re right... the reason I get so fucked up over my dad is because I... love him, i guess... even still...

win.speaker: You
win.text: But then, doesn’t that just mean the reason you’re not messed up at all about Louis... or your husband is because you... don’t... love them...?

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: Don’t put that idea out into the world.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: I loved, love and will continue to love my family until I die.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: ... is that really what it looks like though?

win.speaker: You
win.text: ... a bit...

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: ... When my husband died... I... didn’t deal with it well.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: You weren’t around at the time, but... grief... it changes people.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: It’s a crushing, inescapable feeling that pervades every part of your life.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: I was very depressed, after I got over the shock.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: We knew it was coming, but... part of me didn’t think he’d really die.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: He was in and out of hospitals so much... why would this time be any different?

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: I missed the shock, when it was over.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: The shock softens everything with this air of unreality... detached from the situation.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: I... had a job to do.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: Money to make, bills to pay, a son to raise...

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: I didn’t have time to be depressed, so I... detached.

win.speaker: You
win.text: ... isn’t that just... dissociation?

win.speaker: You
win.text: That’s... bad, isn’t it?

win.speaker: You
win.text: That’s not what... enlightenment or whatever really is.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: ... maybe.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: ...it’s close enough, though, right?

win.speaker: You
win.text: I don’t know if it is...

win.speaker: You
win.text: If... if I try to figure out how to... let things go...

win.speaker: You
win.text: Will you talk to Louis more?

win.speaker: You
win.text: ... I’m worried about him...

win.speaker: You
win.text: I’m kind of worried about you too.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: ... sure, sweetheart.

win.speaker: Thi Há
win.text: Sure.

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: You are tethered to your worldly desires.

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: Angry and spiteful and clinging onto things long gone...

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: This is why you’ve never found yourself at peace.

lose.speaker: You
lose.text: I am having normal human emotions instead of sedating myself with hippy nonsense and very surface level readings of Buddhism!

lose.speaker: You
lose.text: Get your fucking crystal spiritual healing out of my face!

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: Overreliant on the opinions of others, insecure and lashing out.

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: You have a vacuous chasm where your self-esteem should be and you’re angry with me because I’m above your need for assurance.

lose.speaker: You
lose.text: I don’t even know what you’re saying anymore.

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: You need too much, is what I’m saying.

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: Most people need too much.

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: Material possessions, affirmations, human bonds – it’s all ephemeral!

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: The prince Siddhartha Gautama abandoned his royal heritage to a wandering lifestyle after he witnessed human suffering, seeking to end all pain and misery is life.

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: After years of failure, he meditated for 49 days straight, unmoving vigil, and freed himself from the distraction of the mind, becoming Buddha.

lose.speaker: You
lose.text: ... 49 days?

lose.speaker: You
lose.text: No, wait, what’s with the history lesson.

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: It’s the mind and the emotions and the internal temptations that cause you to stray away from the path to enlightenment.

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: Our worldly attachments...

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: You’ll never have peace unless you let it all go.

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: All that’s left is suffering.

lose.speaker: You
lose.text: You’re never going to really talk to Louis, are you?

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: I can guide him

lose.speaker: Thi Há
lose.text: But only he can help himself from the temptations of the mind.

lose.speaker: You
lose.text: I... don’t know why I expected better.
